summary: Terrapin Station → Morning Dew crowns a soaring second set with killer jams
blurb: The June 7, 1977 Winterland show delivers a spectacular second‑set flow – Terrapin Station into Morning Dew, a fiery Scarlet Begonias → Fire → Good Lovin' sequence and an epic Estimated Prophet jam. Jerry’s tone is on fire, Phil locks in tight grooves, and the drums drive the high‑energy peaks.
review: Winterland, June 7 1977, captures the Dead at a creative peak. The first set rolls solidly with highlights like “Peggy‑O,” “Friend Of The Devil,” and an unforgettable seven‑minute “The Music Never Stopped” that sets the stage for the second‑set fireworks. From the seamless segue of “Scarlet Begonias → Fire On The Mountain → Good Lovin’” to the soaring “Estimated Prophet → He’s Gone” jam, the band is locked in; the centerpiece is the transcendent “Terrapin Station → Morning Dew,” a high‑energy climax that feels both tight and exploratory. Jerry Garcia’s jazzy phrasing on “He’s Gone,” his flaming tone on “Morning Dew,” and his vocal fade into the audience are repeatedly praised, while Keith’s keyboard textures (Mutron swirls on “Estimated Prophet”) add depth. The dual‑drum attack of Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann shines in the “Drums → Samson & Delilah” transition, and Phil Lesh provides imaginative bass lines throughout. Minor mix quirks – a brief drop on “Peggy‑O,” slightly muddy vocals on “Scarlet Begonias,” and a few out‑of‑tune guitar moments – don’t detract from what is essentially an iconic 1977 performance.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three of four recordings are clearly positive, repeatedly praising the same highlights (Terrapin>Morning Dew, Estimated Prophet jam, Scarlet/Fire/Good Lovin' sequence). No consistent negative comments about playing; only mixed notes on a few songs. This strong consensus yields a +1.6 sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Soundboard source with good overall balance, captures the standout Terrapin → Morning Dew and other key moments despite minor glitches; widely praised for clarity.

key_highlights: Terrapin Station → Morning Dew second‑set climax
Estimated Prophet jam into He's Gone
Scarlet Begonias > Fire On The Mountain > Good Lovin' seamless segue
The Music Never Stopped extended version in the first set

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers jazzy phrasing on “He’s Gone,” a flaming tone and emotive vocal fade on “Morning Dew,” plus sparkling lead throughout; occasional out‑of‑tune note noted but overall stellar.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides imaginative, solid bass lines, tight start on “Good Lovin’,” and playful interplay during jams; mix occasionally buries his low end but playing remains strong.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s rhythm work and vocal harmonies add beautiful chords to the “Morning Dew” segment; minor rig hiccup in a Funiculi tease but no major impact.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart drives dynamic percussion, especially in the high‑energy “Drums → Samson & Delilah” transition and tight work on “El Paso.”
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming is tight and energetic throughout, complementing Hart and anchoring the second‑set jams.
